title: Cypriot loukoumi
subtitle: Rose-scented Cypriot confection set with cornflour and dusted in icing sugar
excerpt: Cypriot loukoumi is a starch-set confection flavoured with rose water, with a texture that is firmer and more chewy than gelatine-based sweets. It is set purely with cornflour cooked in a sugar syrup over a long, slow simmer. The rose water flavour is added off the heat. Loukoumi is cut into cubes and tossed in a thick coating of cornflour and icing sugar.
sourceNotes: Loukoumi is the Cypriot and Greek form of lokum, closely related to Turkish delight but with its own distinct tradition on the island of Cyprus. Geroskipou loukoumi, from a village near Paphos, is a protected geographical indication in Cyprus. The purely starch-set method, without gelatine, is traditional and gives the distinctive mastic-like chew. This recipe is synthesised from the established Cypriot loukoumi tradition.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Set overnight for the cleanest cut. Once coated in the dusting mix, store in an airtight container at room temperature for up to two weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no baking required; sugar syrup is cooked on the hob

glossaryTerms.definition: Unlike gelatine-based sweets, loukoumi sets through prolonged cooking of cornflour in a sugar syrup. The ratio of cornflour to liquid and the length of cooking time determine the final texture. The mixture must be stirred continuously once the starch slurry is added to the hot syrup or it will scorch on the base. As cooking progresses, the mixture thickens, lightens in colour, and starts to pull away from the sides of the pan. This stage, after about 40 to 45 minutes, is when it is ready. The finished loukoumi has a firmer, denser chew than gelatine-set Turkish delight.

content.text: The sugar syrup reaches 115 degrees C. Keep children away from the hob during cooking. Do not leave the pot unattended once the starch is added.

requires patience and continuous stirring for the full 40 to 45 minutes once the starch is in. This is not a passive recipe. Use a heavy-based pan and a long-handled spoon to protect your arms from spitting.

Oil a 20 cm square tin and line with baking paper. Dissolve the 500 g sugar in 300 ml water in a heavy-based saucepan over low heat. Add the lemon juice. Bring to the boil without stirring and cook to 115 degrees C.

Mix the 150 g cornflour with 400 ml cold water until smooth. Pour this mixture into the hot syrup in a steady stream, whisking constantly. The mixture will thicken and become lumpy immediately; switch to a wooden spoon and stir vigorously to smooth it out. Cook on medium-low heat, stirring continuously, for 40 to 45 minutes until the mixture is very thick, translucent, and pulls away from the sides of the pan when stirred. Remove from the heat and stir in the 3 tbsp rose water. Pour into the prepared tin and smooth the surface. Cool completely at room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 6 hours.

Mix the 50 g cornflour and 60 g icing sugar together. Dust a board generously. Turn out the set loukoumi, peel off the paper and cut into 3 cm squares with an oiled knife. Toss each piece in the dusting mixture until well coated. Store in an airtight container, layered with more dusting mixture.

items.problem: Loukoumi is still very soft after 6 hours
items.solution: The starch mixture was not cooked long enough before setting. It needs the full 40 to 45 minutes on medium-low heat until it pulls away from the pan sides. Refrigerate for a further 6 hours; if still soft, the cooking time was insufficient.

items.problem: Mixture scorched on the base of the pan
items.solution: The heat was too high, or stirring was paused. Use medium-low heat only after adding the starch slurry, and keep stirring without stopping. A heavy-based pan distributes heat more evenly and reduces the risk.
